name: D'Orhy peak
description:
This superb hike leads to the top of the Basque Country while exploring its surroundings. Departing from the Iraty chalets, the round trip itinerary begins with an immersion in the largest beech forest in Europe: the Iraty forest. A true Basque natural treasure, its 20,000 hectares are spread between France and Spain while sheltering a number of remarkable plant and animal species.

The start of the route plunges into the thousand-year-old massif from the Bagargiak pass. The trees quickly part and the ridges of Organbidexka offer a breathtaking view of the Pic d’Orhy. You will continue on a relatively flat portion until you reach the Millagate pass.

Along the way, savor the panoramas that stretch towards the horizon. As you move along a magnificent portion of the ridge, the slope becomes steeper. Be careful not to stray from the main trail. When the peak of Orhy appears on the horizon, there is not much left for you to climb.

Arriving at the summit, you are at the highest point in the Basque Country, at 2,017 meters above sea level. The 360 degree panorama is magnificent. The Atlantic coast, the Basque Country, Béarn, the Pyrenees peaks: your eyes no longer know where to rest. All you have to do is return to the starting point by the same route as on the way out. When you arrive at the Iraty chalets, you will find a restaurant there, but also a shop to perfectly conclude this beautiful hike.
tips: The Iraty chalets are located at Bagargiak Pass at 1,327 meters above sea level. Attached to the village of Larrau, the chalets are located in the beautiful Iraty forest as well as on the GR 10 trail. There you will find something to eat and stock up on water.
Very beautiful ridge a few steps from the Iraty chalets. It offers a superb view of the Pic d'Orhy on the horizon.

The Pic d’Orhy is the highest point in the Basque Country at 2,017 meters above sea level. It offers an incredible panorama of the Bay of Biscay, the Pic d’Anie, Béarn and the first peaks of the Pyrenees. From Larrau, the ascent is along a sharp and arid ridge which remains accessible.
